According to traditional sources, [[Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[Atsuta Shrine]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[Yamato Takeru]] and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=draft}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyasuhime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyasuhime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=draft}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the [[Nara period]], and whose daughter married the semi-legendary [[Emperor Keitai]] and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and [[Emperor Senka]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[Atsuta Shrine]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at [[Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|Seigan-ji]].]]
==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}}
==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== <references />

revision.text:
{{日本の氏族 (古代氏族) |氏名 = 尾張氏 |画像 = |氏姓 = 尾張[[連]]
のち尾張[[宿禰]] |始祖 = 天忍人命 |出自 = |氏祖 = |種別 = [[神別]]（[[天孫]]） |本貫 = [[尾張国]][[愛知郡 (愛知県)|愛知郡]] |人物 = [[世襲足媛]]（[[孝昭天皇]][[皇后]]）
[[宮簀媛]]（[[日本武尊]]妃）
[[尾張大隅]]
[[建稲種命]]
[[尾張草香]]
[[尾張馬身]]
[[尾張兼時]]
[[尾張浜主]] |後裔 = [[熱田神宮|熱田神宮家]]（[[社家]]）
[[海部氏]]（社家）
[[津守氏]]（社家）
[[村国氏]] }} '''尾張氏'''（おわりうじ）は、「尾張」を[[氏]]の名とする[[氏族]]。 『[[日本書紀]]』によると[[天火明命]]（あめのほあかりのみこと）を祖神とし、[[天忍人命]]から始まるとされる{{Cite book|和書|title=日本書紀|pages=巻第二の一書（第六第八）}}。 [[美濃国|美濃]]・[[飛騨国|飛騨]]などに居住の後、[[乎止与命]]のときに[[尾張国造]]となる。[[日本武尊]]（やまとたけるのみこと）の時代には、拠点を[[熱田]]の南に移した。[[宮簀媛]]は日本武尊の妃となり、[[天叢雲剣|草薙神剣]]を熱田に置き、尾張宿彌の後裔の宗族は[[熱田神宮]][[大宮司]]を代々務めた。 尾張[[宿禰]]の裔には、熱田神宮の大内人家・[[権宮司]]・[[八剣宮]]祠官の[[守部氏|守部宿彌]]、[[総検校]]家の[[馬場氏]]、[[祭主]]家の[[田島氏]]がある{{Cite book|和書|title=姓氏家系大辞典|year=1942|publisher=国民社|pages=尾張條、守部條、大喜條}}。また同族には、[[住吉大社]]（[[大阪市]][[住吉区]]）の[[社家]]の[[津守氏]]、そして[[籠神社]]（[[京都府]][[宮津市]]）の[[社家]][[海部氏]]（国宝『[[海部氏系図]]』による）がある。 == 畿内政権からみた尾張氏 == 『[[日本書紀]]』では[[天火明命]]の後裔とされ、皇統譜の古い時期から尾張氏と天皇が結びつき、しばしば后妃を出した[[氏族]]とされる。遠祖である[[奥津余曾]]の妹、[[世襲足媛|余曾多本毘売命]]（世襲足媛）は第5代[[孝昭天皇]]の[[皇后]]となり、子は第6代[[孝安天皇]]となっている。第10代[[崇神天皇]]は[[尾張大海媛]]を妃としている。 [[尾張国造]][[乎止与命]]（をとよ）の子・[[建稲種命]]は[[倭建命]]（ヲウス、日本武尊、第12代[[景行天皇]]の皇子）の東征に副将軍として随伴し軍功を上げた。妹の[[宮簀媛|ミヤズヒメ]]（宮簀媛『日本書紀』、美夜受比売『古事記』）は東征の還路に尾張の地で娶られ、そのときに預かった[[草薙剣]]を奉納し[[熱田神宮|熱田社]]を建てたとある。建稲種命の子の[[尾綱根命]]は応神朝に[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]]として活躍し、その子の[[尾張意乎己]]は仁徳朝の大臣となっている。第15代[[応神天皇]]は建稲種命の孫の[[仲姫命]]を皇后としている。 第26代[[継体天皇]]が大和国入りするまでの正妃は[[尾張連草香]]の娘、目子媛である。第一子の勾大兄皇子は第27代[[安閑天皇]]として、檜隈高田皇子は第28代[[宣化天皇]]として[[即位]]。第30代[[敏達天皇]]と[[皇后]]額田部皇女（第33代[[推古天皇]]）の子に[[尾張皇子]]がいる。 [[壬申の乱]]では海部氏に養育された[[天武天皇|大海人皇子]]へ私邸や資金を提供するなど全面的に支援し、彼の第40代[[天武天皇]]の誕生に格段の功績を上げている。持統天皇10年（[[696年]]）5月条に、[[尾張大隅|尾張宿禰大隅]]が[[位階]]・[[功田]]を授けられる記事が見える。『続日本紀』天平宝字元年（[[757年]]）12月条によれば、この突然の恩賞授与の記事は、[[壬申の乱]]の功績によるものであるという{{要出典|date=2022年3月}}。 == 尾張氏(因幡国) == {{日本の氏族 |家名=尾張氏
（因幡国） |家紋名称= |本姓='''出自不詳''' |家祖= |種別= |出身地=[[因幡国]][[八上郡 (鳥取県)|八上郡]] |根拠地=[[因幡国]][[八上郡 (鳥取県)|八上郡]] ほか |人物= |支流=[[佐治氏]]（[[武家]]）
[[曳田氏]]（[[武家]]） }} [[因幡国]]にも尾張姓を名乗る一族が存在した。この尾張氏は[[八上郡 (鳥取県)|八上]][[郡司]]を務める[[豪族]]で[[平安時代]]末期には一族のうち[[佐治氏]]、[[曳田氏]]が[[開発領主]]として成長した。このうちの佐治氏は[[鎌倉幕府]][[御家人]]となり、[[和田合戦]]で活躍した[[佐治重貞]]は[[佐治氏#佐治郷の開発と支配|佐治郷]][[地頭|地頭職]]に任じられたことが知られている。庶流に比べて尾張氏の本流については詳しく分っていない。 {{-}} == 関連項目 == * [[尾張国]] * [[尾張国造]] * [[断夫山古墳]] * [[熱田神宮]] * [[尾張戸神社]] * [[住吉大社]] * [[津守氏]] * [[津守氏|守部]] * [[津守氏|尾張部]] * [[高倉下]] * [[布都御魂]] {{DEFAULTSORT:おわりうし}} [[Category:尾張氏|!]] [[Category:氏|おわり]] [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:東海地方の氏族]] [[Category:尾張国の氏族]] [[Category:紀伊国の氏族]] [[Category:愛知県の歴史]] [[Category:国造]] [[Category:神別]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} == 出典 == {{Reflist}}

（因幡国） |家紋名称= |本姓='''出自不詳''' |家祖= |種別= |出身地=[[因幡国]][[八上郡 (鳥取県)|八上郡]] |根拠地=[[因幡国]][[八上郡 (鳥取県)|八上郡]] ほか |人物= |支流=[[佐治氏]]（[[武家]]）
[[曳田氏]]（[[武家]]） }} [[因幡国]]にも尾張姓を名乗る一族が存在した。この尾張氏は[[八上郡 (鳥取県)|八上]][[郡司]]を務める[[豪族]]で[[平安時代]]末期には一族のうち[[佐治氏]]、[[曳田氏]]が[[開発領主]]として成長した。このうちの佐治氏は[[鎌倉幕府]][[御家人]]となり、[[和田合戦]]で活躍した[[佐治重貞]]は[[佐治氏#佐治郷の開発と支配|佐治郷]][[地頭|地頭職]]に任じられたことが知られている。庶流に比べて尾張氏の本流については詳しく分っていない。 {{-}} == 関連項目 == * [[尾張国]] * [[尾張国造]] * [[断夫山古墳]] * [[熱田神宮]] * [[尾張戸神社]] * [[住吉大社]] * [[津守氏]] * [[津守氏|守部]] * [[津守氏|尾張部]] * [[高倉下]] * [[布都御魂]] {{DEFAULTSORT:おわりうし}} [[Category:尾張氏|!]] [[Category:氏|おわり]] [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:東海地方の氏族]] [[Category:尾張国の氏族]] [[Category:紀伊国の氏族]] [[Category:愛知県の歴史]] [[Category:国造]] [[Category:神別]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} == 出典 == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[Atsuta Shrine]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|qq=draft}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame-no-hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|qq=draft}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[Heaven]]) to [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|qq=draft}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=draft}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[List of water deities|sea deity]] [[Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of [[Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, [[Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[Atsuta Shrine]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[Yamato Takeru]] and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyasuhime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyasuhime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|qq=draft}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the [[Nara period]], and whose daughter married the semi-legendary [[Emperor Keitai]] and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and [[Emperor Senka]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[Atsuta Shrine]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at [[Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|Seigan-ji]].]]
==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}}

<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan-->

{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[Atsuta Shrine]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|qq=draft}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame-no-hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|qq=draft}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>

Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of [[Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, [[Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. {{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[:en:Heaven|Heaven]]) to [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[:en:List of water deities|sea deity]] [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[:en:Fujiwara no Suenori|Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|qq=|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], and whose daughter married the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at [[:en:Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|Seigan-ji]].]]

<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>

(Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. {{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[:en:Heaven|Heaven]]) to [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[:en:List of water deities|sea deity]] [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[:en:Fujiwara no Suenori|Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|qq=|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], and whose daughter married the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at [[:en:Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|Seigan-ji]].]]

They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].

<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11465311}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[:en:Heaven|Heaven]]) to [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[:en:List of water deities|sea deity]] [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[:en:Fujiwara no Suenori|Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], and whose daughter married the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at [[:en:Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|Seigan-ji]].]]

{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[:en:Heaven|Heaven]]) to [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[:en:List of water deities|sea deity]] [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[:en:Fujiwara no Suenori|Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], and whose daughter married the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

(Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at [[:en:Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|Seigan-ji]].]]

<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11465311}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High [[:en:Heaven|Heaven]]) to [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba]] provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the [[:en:List of water deities|sea deity]] [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[:en:Fujiwara no Suenori|Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], and whose daughter married the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]].<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at [[:en:Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|Seigan-ji]].]]

Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to [[:en:Fujiwara no Suenori|Fujiwara no Suenori]], who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
