In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji}}.]]

[[en:Owari clan]]
[[ja:Owari clan]]
<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11465311}} [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji}}.]]

Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji}}.]]

<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11465311}} [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji}}.]]

{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji}}.]]

<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11465311}} [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari|1=Owari Province}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity|1=List of water deities}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>

Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>

Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}

Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori|1=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>

(Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11465311}} [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Owari clan]][[fa:خاندان اوواری]][[ja:尾張氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari|1=Owari Province}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity|1=List of water deities}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori|1=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji|1=Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)}}.]]

Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori|1=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji|1=Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)}}.]]

[[en:Owari clan]]
[[ja:Owari clan]]
<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--Owari clan--><!--Owari clan--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:خاندان اواری]] [[ja:尾張氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11465311}} [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:Clans of Owari Province]] [[Category:Clans of Tōkai Region]] [[Category:History of Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Owari clan]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{distinguish|Owari Tokugawa family}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|founder=[[Takakuraji]]}} The '''Owari clan''' is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]. They share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
== History ==
=== Origins and genealogy === In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|WD=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari|1=Owari Province}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of the Owari clan, as well as three other clans {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, [[Amabe clan|Amabe]], and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242}}</ref>
However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu|}},'' which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=sea deity|1=List of water deities}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan}}</ref>
=== Atsuta Shrine === The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to [[Miyazu-hime]] and {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}}.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
[[Miyazu-hime]] the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the clan<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref>
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|433}} The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow [[Miyazu-hime]] venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|434}}
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|435}} The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref name="Naito-1975">{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} and [[Miyazu-hime]] and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />{{rp|429}}
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
=== Later history === The "Owariuji keizu" and shrine records reveal that during the times of Emperors Temmu, Jito, and Mommu, Owari no Muraji Inaki and his son, Owari no Muraji Inaoki, were Daiguji. Tadashika held this office for 40 years, from 1045 to 1084. His grandson, Suenori, succeeded him.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Tadanobu's eldest son, Tadayori, was the first Horishi, later called Gonguji. Other positions included Sokengyo and Ouchibito. The Tajima Daiki, and Baba families were known as the Shinkwan.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
Below the Shinkwan were the Negi, also called Churo. Notable families included Awata Mabito, Ohara Mabito, Hayashi Asomi, Matsuoka Mabito, and Kume. Some had rights to worship at Hikami Jinja.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />In 1114, Kazumoto handed the position over to {{ill|Fujiwara no Suenori|en|Fujiwara no Suenori|WD=Q11623142|lt=Fujiwara no Suenori|1=Fujiwara no Suenori}}, who was from the [[Fujiwara clan]].<ref name="Naito-1975" /> Since then, the Fujiwara clan became the head of Atsuta Shrine, while the Owari clan stepped down to the position of adjutant chief priest (''gongūji'').<ref name="Ota-1942">{{Cite book |last=Ota |first=Akira |title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten (姓氏家系大辞典) |publisher=Kokuminsha |year=1942 |volume=1 |pages=1038–1051 |oclc=21114789}}</ref>
In Hoei I (1704), the Daiguji's decision to appoint an Atsuta Hoshi from Jingu-ji led to protests from the Negi families. The protest was not successful.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Cultural influence == According to one theory [[Danpusan Kofun]] is the tomb of a powerful chieftain of the Owari clan, perhaps {{ill|Owari no Kusaka|ja|尾張草香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kusaka|1=Owari no Kusaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465318}}, who is mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, and whose daughter married the semi-legendary {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} and who was the mother of [[Emperor Ankan]] and {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> According to legend [[Miyazu-hime]] a member of the clan is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Owari clan was influential on the development of the [[Kujiki]].<ref name="Aoki-1998" /> It was mainly written as a compilation of elements from the [[Kojiki]], [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]] and [[Kogo Shūi|Kogo Shui]], likely by an author from the [[Mononobe clan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Maxey |first=Trent E. |date=February 2013 |title=John Breen and Mark Teeuwen: A New History of Shinto. (Blackwell Brief Histories of Religion Series.) viii, 264 pp. Chichester: Wiley-Blackwell, 2010. ISBN 978 1 4051 5515 1. |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |journal=Bulletin of the School of Oriental and African Studies |volume=76 |issue=1 |pages=176–177 |doi=10.1017/s0041977x12001796 |s2cid=178230959 |issn=0041-977X}}</ref>{{rp|31}} However it also contains unique elements from neither. Book 5 is believed to preserve traditions of the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and Owari clans not found elsewhere, and Book 10 preserves the earlier historical record the {{Nihongo|Record of the Provincial governors|国造本紀|Kokuzō Hongi}}.<ref name="Aoki-1998">{{cite encyclopedia |year=1998 |title=Sendai Kuji Hongi |encyclopedia=Sekai Dai-Hyakkajiten |publisher=Heibonsha |url=http://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%85%88%E4%BB%A3%E6%97%A7%E4%BA%8B%E6%9C%AC%E7%B4%80 |access-date=July 22, 2013 |last=Aoki |first=Kazuo |author-link=Kazuo Aoki |editor1-last=Katō |editor1-first=Shūichi |editor1-link=Shūichi Katō (critic)}}</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ota-1942"/>[[File:Yoritomo ubuyu.jpg|thumb|227x227px|Monument of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]]'s birthplace, Suenori's villa, at {{ill|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|en|Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)|WD=Q17220718|lt=Seigan-ji|1=Seigan-ji (Atsuta-ku, Nagoya)}}.]]
